---
title: "The Perceptron"
date: "2026-03-14"
author: "Julian M. Del Fiore"
categories: [classification]
---

The perceptron is a classification algorithm, so given an input $x$, we want to produce a label $\hat{y}$.
In particular, it is a supervised model, meaning that we will a labelled training set, where we know the true value $y$ for each sample.

In general, we can choose how to represent $y$.
For binary classification problems, some typical choices include $\{0, 1\}$ and $y\{-1, 1\}$.
Using one or the toher does not affect the generality of the conclusions.
We will alternate between both, selecting the representation that leads to the easiest interpretation in each section.

## Activation function

Similar to what is done in many other models, with the perceptron we use a linear transformation

$$z = wx + b$$

where

 * $x$ is the input, and;
 * $w$ and $b$ are the weight and bias of the model, respectively.

The $z$-value is then passed through an activation function

$$
\activation(z) = \sgn(z) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } z \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{if } z < 0
\end{cases}
$$ {#eq-sign}

![Sign function bla bla](/assets/classification/perceptron/figures/sign.png){#fig-sign width=80%}

which is referred to as the _step_ function due to the shape of its plot, as show in @fig-sign.

## Classification criteria

When we get a new $x$, the objective is to assign it to one class or another, i.e. map it to either $\hat{y} = 0$ or $\hat{y} = 1$.

In the case of the perceptron, this decision is simple: since the output of $\activation(z)$ is already either $0$ or $1$, then the classification criteria is to simply use its value, i.e.,

$$\hat{y} = \activation(z) =
\begin{cases}
1 & \text{if } z \ge 0 \\
0 & \text{if } z < 0
\end{cases}
$$

We can see the decision regions in @fig-decision-region.

![Decision regions](/assets/classification/perceptron/figures/decision_region_zspace.png){#fig-decision-region width=80%}

Note that the decision region lies on the $z$-axis, the shading of the regions in 2D are just to reinforce the idea.

In the $z$-space, it always holds that $z$ is a scalar, i.e., $z \in \mathbb{R}$.
On the contrary, $x$, and thus  $w$ coulb  \in \mathbb{R}^n$.

Hence, we classify based on the value of $z = wx + b$.

```{ojs}
//| echo: false
activation  = z => z >= 0 ? 1 : 0
wValues     = [...d3.range(-8, 0, 0.5), ...d3.range(0.5, 8.5, 0.5)]
viewof wIdx = Inputs.range([0, wValues.length - 1], {value: wValues.indexOf(0.5), step: 1, label: "w", format: i => wValues[i]})
w           = wValues[wIdx]
viewof b    = Inputs.range([-8, 8], {value: 0, step: 1, label: "b"})
```

::: {#fig-decision-boundary}

{{< include ../_shared/decision-plot.qmd >}}

Sigmoid classifier — adjust $w$ and $b$ to explore the decision boundary.

:::

Play with @fig-decision-boundary.

## Training

Recall that this is a supervised algorithm, which means that during training, we get labeled samples, i.e., for all training examples $x$ we get, we know the true label $y$.

Since we get the true labels, we are able to compare the each prediction $\hat{y}$ run by our model with the expected $y$.
This allows us understand how our model is performing at any moment of the training.

The hope is that, iterating, we will learn good values for $w$ and $b$, so that our predictions $\hat{y}$ are mostly correct.
To refine the values of $w$ and $b$, we need to define a loss function.

### Loss function

To know how to optimise the values of $w$ and $b$, we need to define a loss function $\loss$.
Without losing generality, and becauae the formulas look better like this, we will assume $\hat{y} \in \{-1, 1\}$ in this sub-section.


$$
\loss =
$$



The idea behind the perceptron is brute-forcing, a mindset of the type "if there is a solution, I will find it".

For an input $x$ of class $y$, the loss function is



And below some math formulas!

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= ax + b \\
y' &= a
\end{aligned}
$$ {#eq-linear}

See @eq-linear.

$$
\begin{align}
y &= ax + b \\
y' &= a
\end{align}
$$

great!
